text: Unit tests check individual parts of code, like functions or methods, one at a time. Integration tests ensure that different parts of a system work together correctly. In test-driven development, or TDD, you write a failing test first, then code just enough to pass it, and finally refactor. This approach can feel slow at first, but it builds confidence. Each new feature is supported by a safety net of tests. If a change breaks something, you know immediately. Automated tests allow teams to make changes quickly and without fear. When your tests cover both units and integrations, you have strong confidence that the software works as expected, both alone and together.
